content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcer- PRACrrcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o suite 620 r Sacramento, cA g5gl4_232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
December 9,2009
Daniel Abramson
Reed & Davidson, LLP
3699 Wilshire Blvd., Suite 1290
Los Arrgeles, CA 9OOl0-2732
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A.09-246
Dear Mr. Abramson:
This letter is in.response to your request on behalf of Roy Wilson for Supervisor, 2010, a
primarily formed recipient committee that ieeks advice regarding provisions of the political
Reform Act (the "Act") relating to the permissible use of Jurnpuii, ru;;.i- Tli, letter is based
solely-on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of
fact when it renders advice. (In re oglesby (1975) 1 Fppc ops. 71.)
QUESTTON
May Roy Wilson's campaign committee pay the full cost of Supervisor Wilson,s
memorial service, or a portion of the total consisii"g of those costs that would not exist but for
the presence of some 1,000 supporters, constituents, and media representatives whose attendance
is attributable to Mr. Wilson's candidacy?
CONCLUSION
No' The Act does not permit the use of a candidate's campaign funds to pay the costs of
his memorial service after the candidate's untimely death during tir" Iu*paign.
'Tht Polititul Reform Act is contained in Government code Sections g1000 through gl0l4. AII statutory
references are to the Government code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
CommissionarecontainedinSections l8ll0throughlSgglof Title2of thicaliforniacooeoiRegulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of thi California code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-09-246
Page No. 2
Roy Wilson was a candidate for re-election as a Riverside County Supervisor.
Accordingly, he formed a campaign committee, "Roy wilson for Superviror)010,, (hereafter
"the Committee"). Supervisor Wilson died unexpectedly on August 2 6, zOOg. On September Z,
approximately 1,000 supporters and constituentsbf Supirvisor Wilson attended his memorial
seryice, which was also attended by members of the -idiu, along with video and camera crews.
The cost of the service was $18,039.95. That amount is directly attributable to the large number
of persons attending the service, which in tum is due to Supervisor Wilson,s prominence as a
well-respected public official.
FACTS
ANALYSIS
Rules limiting "personal use" of campaign funds are designed to prevent candidates,
elected officials, and others who contro! ttre eipenditure of campai"gn furar'fro- using ;"rry
contributed for political purposes to confer finarrcial benefit on thimselves or other persons. The
general rule is that an expenditure of campaign funds must be reasonably related to a political,
legislative or govemmental purpose. Howevei, where an expenditure of campaign funds confers
a substantial personaf ueryrit 9n any individual or individuals with authoiity to approve the
expenditure of campaign funds held by the committee, the expenditure must be directlyrelated to
a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (sections ssstolt); g95ti.s.;
- 
SiTilarly, section s9.513(b) provides that campaign funds may not be used to reimburse
the cost of professional services unless the servicer u.i dlr""tly relatet to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose. We have long advised that campalgn funds .uy t" used tlo defray
costs. incurred for professional services directly related to activities of the committee which are
consistent with the committee's primary objectives. (See, e.g. the MurphyAdvice Letter, No. A-
e2-4ss.)
We have not previously had occasion to consider the use campaign funds to defray the
cost of a candidate's funeral or memorial service. We begin our analysis 6y noting that the cost
of a funeral or memorial service in our society is normall-y borne uy the f;;iiy or estate of the
deceased, as a personal expense. This -rorm- is clearly reflected in tfre federal regulation that
defines "personal use" of campaign funds under the Federal Election Cu-puilr* Act, the federal
counterpart of our own Act. Specifically, 11 CFR Section l13.l(g) provides,In pertinent part:
"(g) Personal use. personal use means any use of funds in a
campaign account of a present or former candidate to fulfill a
commitment, obligation or expense of any person that would exist
irrespective of the candidate's campaign or duties as a federal
officeholder.
"(l) Personal use includes but is not limited to the use of funds in
a campaign account for any item listed in paragraphs (gXl)(A)
through (J) of rhis section:
"(A) Household food items or supplies


File No. A-09-246
Page No. 3
"(B) Funeral, cremation or burial expenses except those incurred
by a candidate (as defined in ll cFR 100.3) oi* employee or
volunteer of an authorized committee whose death arises ouiol o.
in the course of, campaign activity.,,
Sections 89510 through 89522 the Act describes permissible uses of campaign funds,
which are made subject at the outset ro a rrust described by Section g9510(b):
"(b) All contributions deposited into the campaign account shall be
deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated with the election
of the candidate or for expenses associated with holding office."
The Act does not expressly permit use of a candidate's campaign funds to defray
expenses of a candidate's funeral or memorial service. But unlike iis fideral counterpart, the Act
does not offer a full, itemized list of prohibited uses. We therefore ask whether the committee,s
payment towards the cost of Supervisor Wilson's memorial service would be an expenditure
associated with seeking or holding office, as defined by Section g9512:
"An expenditure to seek office is within the lawful execution of the
trust imposed by Section 89510 if it is reasonably related to a
political purpose. An expenditure associated with trotaing office is
within the lawful execution of the trust imposed by Sect[n g9510
if it is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose.
Expenditures which confer a substantial peisonal benetit shall be
directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose."2
As noted above, we have advised that campaign funds may be used to pay for
professional services directly related to activities 
^ 
lrihether 'poliiical,,, Iegislative,,, or
"governmental") of the committee which are consistent *ith the committee,s primary objectives.
But we cannot conclude that a payment for the memorial service is related to the committee,s
primary objective - securing the Supervisor's re-election. Nor can we conclude that such a
payment2 might be classified as an expenditure associated with holding thai office after the
election'r Given the cost of the *"-oiiul service, payment by the committee would confer a
2 section s95I I(bX3) defines "substantial personal benefit" as an expenditure ofcampaign funds resulting
in a direct personal benefit of more than $200 to thi candidate, elected 9fficer, or any individuit *irr, authority to
approve expenditure of the committee's campaign funds. Regularion 18960(aXl) furthei.^piuirr rhat an
expenditure of campaign funds results in a direct personal benefit when, within six months oi the expenditure, the
candidate' elected officer, or a member of his or-her immediate familyiealizes an increase or decrease in income or
assets, or a decrease in expenses or liabilities, of more than $200. The term ..immediate family; is defined at
Section 82029 to include a spouse or a dependant child.
. 
3 You correctly observe that the Act expres.sly creates exceptions at Section g95 l3(0(3) which deem certain
campaign expenditures to be directly related to i political, legislativf, or governmental purpose. But we are
constrained by settled principles of statutory consiruction, un-a uy Section8l003, to conitrue exceptions to the
"personal use" statutes narrowly. without direction by the Commission, we cannot advise that it is possible to


File No. A-09-246
Page No.4
personal benefit on pY person or persons otherwise liable to pay for it, but it would not be an
expenditure within the lawful execution of the trust imposed by Section S9510(b) on the use of
campaign funds.
we recognize that this advice may seem harsh under circumstances already tragic. But
our reading of the law is that it simply does 
^not contemplate the substitution of campaign funds
for the personal funds ordinarily used to defray the cosis of a candidate,s funeral or memorial
service' This is the rule expressly provided by parallel federal law, and is the rule evidently
presumed throughout the Act's long history.
Pursuant to Regulation 18951(a)(3), funds in the committee become .,surplus funds,, on
December 31,2009. As pertinent here, surplus campaign funds may be spent uy the committee
as provided by Section 89519(b):
:(b) Surplus campaign funds shall be used only for the
following purposes:
"q^) The payment of outstanding campaign debts or elected
officer's expenses.
"(2) The repayment of contributions.
"(3) Donations to any bona fide charitable, educational, civic,
religious, or similar tax-exempt, nonprofit organization, where no
substantial part of the proceeds will have a material financial effect
on the former candidate or elected officer, any member of his or
her immediate family, or his or her campaign trlasurer.
"(4) contributions to a political party committee, provided the
campaign funds are not used to support or oppose candidates for
elective office. However, the campiign runas may be used by a
political party committee to conduct partisan voter registration,
partisan get-out-the-vote activities, and ilate maile., u. th"ut term is
defined in Section 82048.3.
"(5) contributions to support or oppose any candidate for federal
office, any candidate for elective office in u ,tut" other than
California, or any ballot measure.
"(6) The payment for professional services reasonably required by
the committee to assist in the performance of its administrative
functions, including payment ior attorney's fees for ritigation
which arises directly out of a candidat"i, o, elected officer,s
activities, duties, or status as a candidate or elected officer,
including, but not limited to, an action to enjoin defamation,
defense of an action brought of a violation of state or rocal
campaign, disclosure, or election laws, and an action from an
election contest or recount."
extend so broadly exceptions Iimited
purchase of relatively small gifts for
to the funding of campaign cerebrations and simirar campaign events, or the
campaign workers.


File No. A-09-246
Page No. 5
We do not see any basis in Section 89519 for concluding that campaign funds may be
used after December 3l to pay costs of the memorial service which they couii not have lawfully
paid before becoming "surplus.,'
We understand and accept your point that the costs of the memorial service are
attributable, at least in part, to the esteem that Supervisor Wilson had earned from his
constituents by years of exemplary public service. But this fact does not entitle us to read the
Act in a manner that would permit campaign funds to subsidize expenses ordinarily borne by
personal resources.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General CounselLlttu
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
Lrwjgl

is attributable to Mr. Wilson's candidacy?

CONCLUSION: No' The Act does not permit the use of a candidate's campaign funds to pay the costs of
